channel.title: things i am currently doing re: computers
channel.description: RSS feed for things i am currently doing re: computers

item.title: Making Pull Requests
Loading A Dtd From A Jar
item.description: Bundling resources into a jar file is a common way to deploy your Java application, useful because it makes the jar's resources available to retrieve via the classloader at runtime.

item.title: Writing Libraries In Bash
item.description: <!-- ![BY-NC-SA shells by Cody Geary](https://farm1.staticflickr.com/119/308384875_5232c39c9a_n.jpg) -->

item.title: Getting Ocmock To Play Nicely With Xcode 5
item.description: I've started writing a dashboard Cocoa app, and I've found the process of setting up OCMock really unreasonably painful.

item.title: Switching Text Editors
item.description: After 7 years, I'm finally doing the dirty dead: jumping off the Vim ship, into the [adjective], [adjective] something [noun] that is emacs. There follows a brief description of the process so far.

item.title: Using Mockito As A Crutch
item.description: "omne trium perfectum", as the Latins used to say: a set of three is perfect. Sadly I don't have three facts I know about mockito, but I do have two facts. That's not enough to impress anyone, which means almost by definition, it's enough for a blog.

item.title: Hanging Out With Linked Hash Map
item.description: LinkedHashMap is a surprising useful data structure, and missing from many standard libraries.

item.title: Chopping Down Tall Classes
item.description: I've been having bags of fun today trying to get to grips with an enormously busy class. The carbuncle of code is basically a data structure that contains a tree of values loaded from a plain-text configuration file. To keep it from being too simple and boring, somebody decided you should be able to pass it an instance of our application, and have it call lots of configuration methods on it for you.

item.title: Pressing Buttons In Bash
item.description: Shortcuts are a source of constant amusement as far as I'm concerned, and nothing on Earth makes me more ecstatically happy than accidentally discovering a new one. Today's mis-fingering was ^t in bash, which roughly translated means transpose the character under the cursor and the previous character.

item.title: Writing A Simple Gradle Build
item.description: 'Build as code' is one of those dreadful smells you start to recognise after a while, so gradle, which is a highly free-form system, is not my first choice for complex builds, but it is a beautiful way to build a simple set of modules. I've written something along the lines of the following build enough times for me to make a note of it for future copy-pasting, so here it is.

item.title: Deleting Files From Git Object Store
item.description: Tonight I accidentally did something stupid and committed binaries to my git repository, despite knowing better in the first place. After the commit I tried to push.

item.title: Defusing Xml
Blogging With Pelican
Bringing Eclipse Shortcuts To Xcode
item.description: When I first arrived in the real world, I was introduced to new things; amongst these were 'collaboration' and 'gigantic code bases'. I grudgingly accepted the need for an IDE, and started to use Eclipse. There are actions I use in Eclipse regularly that I find really useful, like searching and manipulating text.

item.title: Disable Cache In Chrome And Other Goodies
item.description: Through some lucky keyboard-mashing I stumbled across a couple of useful developer features in Google Chrome, including an option to disable the cache, and settings for device testing. Here's how to:

item.title: Code Shame
item.description: Naturally when I write C I'm reasonably careful about a number of things. Because at runtime it's a free-for-all, I take care to notice buffer overruns, uninitialized memory, and in particular the possible values assigned to a pointer.

item.title: Lazy Instantiation In Objective C
item.description: Lazy instantiation is a relatively cheap way of allocating memory to component objects as it's required instead of up-front in a constructor.

item.title: Deploying An Xml File With Maven
item.description: Something clever we've started doing at work is publishing common build files to our local maven repository, so builds can depend on known versions of scripts, rather than just the latest from source control.

item.title: Fixing A Permissions Error Opening A Semaphore
item.description: Today I spent a couple of hours getting semaphores to work.

item.title: Reading From Sockets
item.description: This is a story about how I learned to read streams from sockets, or to put it another way:

item.title: Saving The Workspace From An Eclipse Plugin
item.description: Since I'm going to the trouble of making a plugin for Eclipse, I thought I may as well try and make if look and feel a bit Eclipsy, so when it came to writing an export feature to bundle the user's project, I decided it would be a jolly good thing if the user got a dialog to prompt them to save their changes, much as when you run an application in Eclipse.

item.title: Fixing Git Upload Pack Not Found
item.description: I've only just realised that blogging about problems is a great way of delaying doing the thing I intended to get done in the first place.

item.title: Monitor A Long Running Process With Control T
item.description: One of the nice things about being in Linux exile is learning about new things that I hadn't noticed were missing from my life, like being able to tell if a process has hung, or it's just taking a really long time.

item.title: Building An Eclipse Plugin With Tycho
item.description: I spent a couple of days this week converting the build of an Eclipse plugin project from Ant with Ivy to Maven using the Tycho plugins. I'll demonstrate the build with an example project.

item.title: Unit Testing In C With Check Setting Up A Project
item.description: In a previous post I went through the steps to run the Check tutorial, had a bit of a whinge about how difficult is had all been, and mumbled a bit about coming back when I'd figured out how to make everything work on a real project. In fact, that was much simpler than I expected.

item.title: To Sudo Or Not To Sudo
item.description: This is a pretty neat trick I've just seen in a tutorial from the chaps at opscode.

item.title: Unit Testing In C With Check
item.description:
Pushy

I've been developing a cheerfully indolent little HTTP server for some time now, as one of those 'learn-more-c', 'learn-TDD' projects that I imagine nearly everyone has lying around, half neglected.

item.title: Unpacking Maven Dependencies
item.description: Unpacking a kit for inclusion in a Java library

item.title: Using Jvm Shutdown Hooks To Kill A Child Process
item.description: Today I spent some time writing a runner for a command-line Java socket application. I'd assumed that it would be easy enough to quit the process by hitting ^C. I'm here to report that this is not the case.

item.title: Disabling The Next Button In An Eclipse Wizard
item.description: I've been writing an Eclipse wizard that will be used to create new socket applications. The options selected in early in the wizard determine whether it's appropriate for the user to configure connection properties before creating the project.

item.title: Modular Projects With Bash
item.description: I try to use bash scripts wherever I can over ruby or python. Partly that's down to stubbornness, but it's mostly because the problems I'm trying to solve can be tackled with the tools already available in UNIX.
